prep.notes: watch.py exited 0; report at scratchpad/confirm/vZdbbN3FCzE/control_report.txt (52,061 bytes); 100 JPEG frames in control/frames/; existing transcript.txt copied to eval/transcript.txt (17,998 chars), not regenerated.

ctlAnswers.answer:
This is a video essay (by Marques Brownlee / MKBHD, per the on-screen YouTube review cards credited to "Marques Brownlee" and his mention of MKBHD.com at [14:48]) about the full arc of OnePlus from enthusiast darling to mainstream brand.

Main points:

1. Framing: An article headlined "EXCLUSIVE: OnePlus is being dismantled" circulated, but it contained no exclusive information, was mostly speculation, and read like it was written by ChatGPT [00:03]-[00:20]. It prompted the host to reflect on "the arc of OnePlus" [00:26]-[00:29]. He cites an ~8-year-old Techaltar video, "Why Enthusiast Brands Will Betray You" [00:30]-[00:37]; a frame around [00:34]-[00:45] shows on-screen that video's title card plus a Lifehacker article headlined "CyanogenMod Is Dead, and Its Successor is Lineage OS."

2. Thesis: Companies keep following the arc of starting enthusiast-focused and then turning, and many die making the leap from enthusiast success to mass-market brand (Nextbit, Essential, ASUS are named; frames around [01:05]-[01:16] show the Essential Phone and ASUS ROG Phone) [00:43]-[01:09]. OnePlus is still alive in 2026, has completed the whole arc, and has "accidentally become the blueprint for survival as an enthusiast brand," with betrayal as part of that blueprint [01:14]-[01:32].

3. Origin: Breaking into the US smartphone market is basically impossible; it has been a Samsung/Apple duopoly, with LG/HTC/Motorola fighting over scraps in 2014 (a frame near [01:50] shows a Galaxy S5) [01:35]-[01:57]. Then the OnePlus One launched with huge hype: flagship specs (latest-gen Snapdragon 800-series; an on-screen graphic near [02:13] lists "2.5GHz Quad[-core] Snapdragon, Adreno 330, 3GB of RAM" over the phone booting CyanogenMod 11.0), a $300 price, sandstone design, and clean, developer-friendly CyanogenMod software; it was dubbed the "flagship killer" versus $600-700 Apple/Samsung flagships [01:58]-[02:39]. Despite an invite-only purchase system, it sold over a million units and put OnePlus on the map: "right recipe, right time" [02:49]-[03:02].

4. The dilemma: OnePlus captured "the exact worst customer group imaginable": enthusiasts, who are the most hyped but pickiest, least loyal, and smallest group [03:05]-[03:27]. A technology-adoption-curve chart on screen around [03:30]-[03:37] labels Innovators 2.5%, Early Adopters 13.5%, Early Majority 34%, Late Majority 34%, Laggards 16%, with "The Chasm" between the early market and the mainstream market [03:30]-[03:43].

5. Second generation (an on-screen chapter card "Startup Phase 1" appears near the [03:45] section break): the OnePlus One deliberately skipped things like an official IP rating and advertising to pass savings to users, which reviewers loved [04:00]-[04:22]. The OnePlus 2 played it safe with spec bumps and a small price bump to $330, but the $250 mid-range, designer-style OnePlus X was a "wobble" that enthusiasts didn't want; the OnePlus 3/3T returned to enthusiast form [04:23]-[05:15].

6. Peak OnePlus (for enthusiasts): around the OnePlus 5/6, on top of the world by the 7 series, with OxygenOS (post-CyanogenMod) as super smooth optimized software; reviewers broadly agreed these were the best bang-for-buck in the industry [05:21]-[06:07]. His own review titles reflect it: OnePlus 6 "Right on the Money" (an on-screen YouTube card near [06:23] shows "OnePlus 6 Review: Right On the Money!" with 22M views), OnePlus 7 "Under the Radar," 7 Pro "Silly Fast!", 7T Pro "Tiny Tweaks to Excellence"; the $660 OnePlus 7T Pro is his favorite OnePlus ever [06:19]-[06:57]. But the masses still weren't buying; most people buy safe options in carrier stores every 3-4 years [06:58]-[07:26]. If OnePlus could cross that gap (the chasm chart reappears on screen around [07:17]-[07:30]), it could break the Samsung/Apple duopoly, scale in India, or land US carrier deals [07:26]-[07:53].

7. The core tension: everything that won enthusiasts (cost-cutting, spec-maxing, skipping the IP rating, no commercials or carrier deals, direct-to-consumer prices) is the exact opposite of what the masses want, and offering what the masses want drives enthusiasts away [08:03]-[09:18]. Every enthusiast brand hits this realization [09:19]-[09:24]. Failed examples: ASUS's ROG Phone was slowly "neutered" toward mainstream features like wireless charging and is now dead [09:36]-[10:05], and the beloved small Zenfone 9/10 pivoted abruptly to the huge Zenfone 11 Ultra, which went "terribly" because masses want big screens [10:06]-[10:35]. The rule: you must build the new mainstream audience faster than you lose the old enthusiast one [10:38]-[10:47].

8. OnePlus's transition (an on-screen chapter card "4 Realization" appears near this section, around [10:48]-[11:40]): his OnePlus 8 review was titled "Special. No More" (on-screen YouTube card near [10:51]-[11:00] shows "OnePlus 8 Review: Special No More!", 3.3M views) [10:51]-[11:00]. OnePlus pulled off "the most successful, but also least successful jump from enthusiast to mainstream that we've ever seen" [11:01]-[11:09]: all-glass designs, official IP ratings, prices creeping up "like slowly turning up the temperature on a stove" to $900 phones, the budget Nord line, US carrier deals, co-founder Carl Pei leaving, and the Hasselblad camera partnership starting with the OnePlus 9 (a frame near [12:00] shows a Hasselblad-branded OnePlus 9-series camera module) [11:10]-[12:09]. By the OnePlus 10 they'd almost completely lost their identity, resembling cousin-company Oppo's phones, with OxygenOS fading to resemble Oppo's ColorOS [12:09]-[12:48].

9. Reconciling "peak": the video explicitly gives two peaks; for enthusiasts it was around the OnePlus 5/6 [05:26] (later restated as OnePlus 6/7 [12:18]-[12:22]), while for OnePlus's own books it was probably the OnePlus 10 with a big product lineup, even though nothing stood out anymore [12:23]-[12:31].

10. Today and the verdict: the OnePlus 15 is a solid daily phone (smooth, great battery, fast charging) but generic-looking with mediocre cameras; enthusiasts long ago moved on to Pocophones, Sony Xperia, Nothing, etc., yet it's now a phone recommendable to everyday people [12:48]-[13:13]. "Success?? Technically. Barely." [13:20]-[13:23]. The argument about the trajectory: OnePlus's arc "will be studied" precisely because they survived; the survival blueprint is a very deliberate, slow peeling back of enthusiast edges and softening while not losing enthusiasts too fast and building mainstream appeal [13:24]-[14:06]. Ironically, Carl Pei seems to be "speedrunning the same process" at Nothing [14:06]-[14:13]. The future is uncertain (there is a rumor the next foldable and the compact-flagship sequel were cancelled, though he expects a OnePlus 16 this year) [14:17]-[14:31]; this reconciles with the opening: the "dismantled" headline was unsupported speculation [00:12]-[00:18], while the video's verified claim is only that OnePlus is "still alive" with an uncertain future [13:27]-[14:21]. Closing warning: "enthusiasts do not forget" [14:44], followed by an MKBHD.com sweatshirt plug [14:46]-[14:51].

Launch price: The original OnePlus One cost $300 at launch. The narrator says that before it even started shipping "it had all these flagship specs with the latest-gen Snapdragon 800 series chip, and it was $300 somehow, which was unreal" [02:13]-[02:17].

What made it a hit with enthusiasts (all from this video's evidence):

1. Flagship specs at that low price: the latest-gen Snapdragon 800-series chip [02:13]. An on-screen spec graphic (frame shown around [02:13], with the phone displaying a CyanogenMod 11.0 boot screen) lists "2.5GHz Quad[-core] Snapdragon, Adreno 330, 3GB of RAM"; another frame around [02:55]-[02:58] shows the phone running Android 4.4.2 (KitKat).
2. Clean design with the new "sandstone" back texture [02:19]; frames near [01:58]-[02:05] and [13:31] show the phone's white back with the OnePlus logo.
3. Clean, enthusiast-friendly software: it ran CyanogenMod, a "developer-friendly, clean, no-bloatware experience" described as "a breath of fresh air" [02:23]-[02:30].
4. The "flagship killer" value proposition: the enthusiast mindset was, why spend $600 or $700 on Apple or Samsung flagships when you could get this [02:32]-[02:42].
5. Deliberate cost-cutting tradeoffs that enthusiasts loved: OnePlus actively left out things mainstream phones had, like an official IP rating and any advertising, so the savings could be passed to the end user; "tech heads, including me, we raved about this. We loved it. We ate it up" [04:00]-[04:22]. This is reiterated later: making the phone effectively waterproof but skipping the official IP-rating fee was "Legendary!", and selling directly to consumers at lower prices without commercials or carrier deals was "Perfect" for enthusiasts [08:48]-[09:04].
6. Huge hype and scarcity: "The hype for this phone was absolutely nuts" before shipping [02:09]-[02:11], and you needed an invite to buy one under a system that was "crazy hard to get" [02:48]-[02:52]. Despite that, it sold over a million units and put OnePlus "immediately on the map": "right recipe, right time" [02:53]-[03:02].

The video also notes the flip side of this hit: only enthusiasts were going to find the OnePlus One in the first place [02:42]-[02:46], and it is later called "the ultimate pure enthusiast phone" [13:31]-[13:36].

One clarification to avoid confusion (not a conflict): other prices mentioned in the video refer to different models, not the One: the OnePlus 2 was bumped "up just a tiny bit to $330" [04:30]-[04:33] and the OnePlus X was a $250 mid-range phone [04:46]-[04:47]. The One itself is consistently described only at $300 [02:17].

evAnswers.answer:
This is an MKBHD video using OnePlus's full history as a case study of the 'enthusiast brand arc': how a company that wins over tech enthusiasts must eventually betray them to reach the mainstream, and how OnePlus is the rare company that survived that jump, barely.

Main points:

1. Framing: The video was prompted by an article headlined 'EXCLUSIVE: OnePlus is being dismantled' [00:07], which turned out to contain no exclusive information, just speculation [00:12]. An on-screen frame at [00:10] shows the actual post: an Androidheadline tweet reading 'EXCLUSIVE: OnePlus Is Being Dismantled' over a OnePlus 'Never Settle' image. This got him thinking about 'the arc of OnePlus' [00:29] and a Techaltar video about why 'enthusiast brands will always betray you' [00:34]. Many companies die on the leap from enthusiast success to mass appeal, e.g. Nextbit, Essential, ASUS [00:54, 01:05], but OnePlus is 'still alive here in 2026' and has 'accidentally become the blueprint for survival as an enthusiast brand,' with betrayal as part of that blueprint [01:14, 01:20, 01:28].

2. Startup phase: In 2014 the US market was a Samsung/Apple duopoly [01:44, 01:50]. The OnePlus One broke in with flagship specs (latest-gen Snapdragon 800-series chip) at $300 [02:13, 02:17], a clean sandstone design [02:19], and clean CyanogenMod software [02:25], earning the 'flagship killer' label versus $600-700 flagships [02:32]. An on-screen spec overlay at [02:07] lists '2.5GHz Quad-Core Snapdragon 8..., Adreno 330 G[PU], 3GB of RAM' next to a phone booting CyanogenMod 11.0. Despite an invite-only purchase system [02:49], it sold over a million units [02:54]. But this captured 'the exact worst customer group imaginable: the enthusiast' [03:05], who are picky, high-standards, least loyal, and the smallest group [03:16-03:27]. A frame at [03:31] shows a technology-adoption-curve graphic on screen (Tech Enthusiasts/Innovators 2.5%, Visionaries/Early Adopters 13.5%, Early Majority 34%), illustrating how small that audience is. 'This is not sustainable, but... it's a hell of a start' [03:41].

3. Generation 2: The second generation shows what a company learned [03:49]. The OnePlus 2 (shown in hand at [04:28]) kept the formula with spec bumps and a small price bump to $330 [04:28-04:33], but the OnePlus X, a $250 mid-range 'designer' phone, was a 'little wobble' that enthusiasts didn't want [04:35-04:55, 05:00]. The OnePlus 3/3T went back to enthusiast phones [05:05].

4. Peak: Peak OnePlus for enthusiasts was around the OnePlus 5 and 6 (a OnePlus 5 is shown at [05:27]), and they were 'on top of the world' by the OnePlus 7 series [05:26, 05:29]. OxygenOS (settings shown on-device at [05:53]) had become super smooth, optimized software [05:45], and reviewers agreed these were 'the best bang for the buck in the entire industry' [06:04]. His own review titles reflected it ('Right on the Money,' 'Under the Radar,' 'Silly Fast!,' 'Tiny Tweaks to Excellence') [06:23-06:28]; his favorite was the $660 OnePlus 7T Pro [06:34, 06:54] (a OnePlus 7 Pro appears at [06:49] and [08:42]). But the masses still weren't buying, they buy safe options in carrier stores every 3-4 years [07:00, 07:19]. A frame at [07:17] shows the full adoption-curve graphic labeled 'THE CHASM' between the Early Market and Mainstream Market, the gap OnePlus needed to cross to break the Samsung/Apple duopoly or scale in markets like India [07:32-07:47].

5. Realization (the core argument): What wins enthusiasts is 'the exact opposite of what the masses want' [08:34-08:37]. Cost-cutting like skipping the official IP rating and avoiding commercials/carrier deals was 'legendary' to enthusiasts [08:48-09:01], but the masses want the safe, IP-rated phone on the carrier shelf [09:07-09:09], so serving the masses 'drives the enthusiasts away' [09:11-09:17]. Every enthusiast brand hits this realization [09:19]. Cautionary examples: the ASUS ROG Phone was slowly 'neutered' toward the mainstream and is now dead [09:38-10:05] (a ROG Phone appears earlier at [01:10]), and the ASUS Zenfone tried a sudden jump from the beloved small Zenfone 9/10 to the huge Zenfone 11 Ultra, which went 'terribly' [10:06-10:33]; a dark product-launch shot appears at [10:33] during this point. The rule: you must build the new mainstream audience faster than you lose the old one [10:38-10:46].

6. The Jump: His OnePlus 8 review title was 'Special. No More.' [10:51], and a frame at [10:56] shows his own YouTube video 'OnePlus 10 Pro Impressions: What Happened?' on screen. OnePlus pulled off 'the most successful, but also least successful jump from enthusiast to mainstream that we've ever seen' [11:01-11:07]. They adopted glass designs, added official IP ratings, raised prices to $900 'like slowly turning up the temperature on a stove' [11:13-11:32] (a OnePlus flagship on a desk is shown at [11:32]), added the budget Nord line [11:33], made US carrier deals [11:45], lost co-founder Carl Pei [11:56], and partnered with Hasselblad from the OnePlus 9 series [12:00] (a Hasselblad-branded camera module is shown at [12:00]). By the OnePlus 10 they had 'almost completely lost their identity,' resembling cousin company Oppo's phones [12:09-12:16].

7. Verdict on the trajectory: He explicitly reconciles two views of 'peak': for enthusiasts it was OnePlus 6/7, but for OnePlus's own books it was probably OnePlus 10 with its big lineup [12:18-12:29]. Today's OnePlus 15 (three colorways shown at [12:56]) is a solid, smooth phone with great battery and fast charging but generic design and mediocre cameras [12:48-13:02]; enthusiasts have long moved on to Pocophone, Sony Xperia, Nothing [13:05-13:11], while it's now recommendable to everyday people [13:13]. He also reconciles the seeming contradiction of success vs downfall directly: 'success?? Technically. Barely.' [13:20-13:23] and 'the downfall of OnePlus will be studied, because they are still alive' [13:24-13:27]. The survival path is deliberately, slowly peeling back the enthusiast edges without losing that audience too fast [13:46-14:03]. Ironically, Carl Pei seems to be 'speedrunning the same process with Nothing' [14:07]. The future is uncertain (rumors of a canceled foldable and canceled compact-flagship sequel, though a OnePlus 16 is likely) [14:21-14:28], but the arc 'will have to be studied' by anyone attempting the same jump, because 'enthusiasts do not forget' [14:33-14:44].

Launch price: The original OnePlus One cost $300 at launch. The narrator says it 'had all these flagship specs with the latest-gen Snapdragon 800 series chip, and it was $300 somehow, which was unreal' [02:13, 02:17]. This is consistent with a later statement that the follow-up OnePlus 2 'bumped the price up just a tiny bit to $330' [04:30-04:33], so there is no conflict in the evidence about the $300 figure.

What made it a hit with enthusiasts (all in the context of the 2014 US market being a Samsung/Apple duopoly with LG, HTC, and Motorola fighting over scraps [01:50-01:58]):

1. Flagship specs at a third to half the flagship price: latest-generation Snapdragon 800-series chip for $300 [02:13, 02:17], versus the $600-700 Apple and Samsung flagships. That value gap earned it the 'flagship killer' nickname: 'why would anyone spend $600 or $700 on the Apple or Samsung flagships anymore when you can get this? At least, that's what the enthusiast mindset was' [02:32-02:39].

2. Clean design: 'this pretty clean design with this little new sandstone thing going on' [02:19].

3. Clean, developer-friendly software: it ran CyanogenMod, 'this developer-friendly, clean, no-bloatware experience... it was a breath of fresh air' [02:25-02:30].

4. Enthusiast-targeted tradeoffs: they deliberately left out things mainstream phones had, like an official IP rating and any advertising, to save money that was 'passed down to the end-user,' and 'tech heads, including me, we raved about this. We loved it. We ate it up' [04:09-04:22].

5. Hype and scarcity: 'The hype for this phone was absolutely nuts' before it even shipped [02:09-02:11], and you needed an invite to buy one, a system that was 'crazy hard to get' [02:49-02:52]. Despite that, they sold over a million units and 'were immediately on the map', 'right recipe, right time' [02:54-02:59].

The narrator later sums the phone up as 'the ultimate pure enthusiast phone' [13:31]. He also notes the flip side: this hit captured 'the exact worst customer group imaginable: the enthusiast' (picky, highest standards, least loyal, and the smallest group), so the success was 'not sustainable, but... a hell of a start' [03:05-03:43]; an on-screen adoption-curve graphic visible in the frame at [07:17] (labeled 'THE CHASM,' with Tech Enthusiasts/Innovators at 2.5% and Early Adopters at 13.5% versus a 34%+34% mainstream majority) illustrates how small that enthusiast group is.

Note on the visual evidence: none of the supplied frames actually shows the OnePlus One itself or its price on screen; the frames show the presenter speaking [01:43, 03:56, 08:08], the 'OnePlus is being dismantled' Androidheadline tweet [00:10], a later OnePlus 5 in hand [05:27], the adoption-curve chart [07:17], a dark product-launch shot [10:33], and a YouTube thumbnail for 'OnePlus 10 Pro Impressions: What Happened?' [10:56]. The $300 price and the hit factors are therefore established by the spoken transcript, not on-screen text.

per_question.rationale: Both cover all 4 gold facts and every spot-checked citation verified against the transcript (A: 02:17 $300, 04:33 $330, 11:31 $900, 06:23 review title, 14:44; B: 02:54 million units, 11:45 carrier deals, 11:56 Carl Pei, 12:00 Hasselblad, 14:07 Nothing). A is marginally more complete: it includes the OxygenOS-fading-to-ColorOS detail [12:41]-[12:46], the explicit 'masses want big screens' reason for the Zenfone 11 Ultra failure [10:23]-[10:25], and explicitly reconciles the opening 'dismantled' article with the closing 'still alive but uncertain' verdict. B omits the ColorOS point and states the Zenfone failure without its stated cause. A's blemish is a stack of very specific unverifiable visual claims (view counts '22M'/'3.3M', chapter-card names), permitted as plausibly-visual but adding risk without value; this keeps A at 9 rather than 10. Neither answer contradicts the transcript anywhere.

Both give the transcript-correct $300 launch price [02:17] and cover all 4 gold facts (flagship specs vs $600-700 rivals, invite scarcity + hype, CyanogenMod software freedom). All spot-checked citations verified for both (A: 02:19 sandstone, 02:23-02:30 CyanogenMod, 08:48-09:04 IP-skip 'Legendary'; B: 02:32-02:39 flagship killer, 04:09-04:22 deliberate omissions, 13:31 'ultimate pure enthusiast phone'). A wins narrowly on precision: it adds a useful disambiguation of the other prices in the video ($330 OnePlus 2, $250 OnePlus X, both correctly cited) and has zero checkable inaccuracies. B contains one mild overstatement: 'a third to half the flagship price' ($300 vs $600-700 is 43-50%; the transcript supports 'half', not 'a third'). B earns credit for its honest note separating transcript-grounded facts from frame-grounded evidence, which is why it stays at 8 despite the imprecision.

Both cover all 4 gold facts: 2014 launch at $300 (transcript says $300, not $299) undercutting $600-700 flagships; invite system + million-unit enthusiast launch; price creep ($330 -> $900 'stove' metaphor) plus Nord line and lineup sprawl; and the betrayal-arc argument that OnePlus abandoned its enthusiast identity to become a mainstream $900-phone maker resembling Oppo. Spot-checked 6+ citations per answer against the transcript; all verified ([02:17] $300, [04:33] $330, [10:51] 'Special. No More', [11:45] carrier deals, [12:00] Hasselblad, [13:24-13:27] 'downfall will be studied'). A is slightly more granular (explicit dual-peak reconciliation at [05:26] vs [12:23-12:31], reconciles the closing with the opening 'dismantled' headline); B opens with a crisper thesis statement directly answering the trajectory question and reconciles the same dual-peak point. Both lean on plausible-but-unverifiable visual claims (spec overlays, adoption-curve chart, review cards), none contradicted by the transcript. Quality is effectively equal.

Both state the $300 launch price with the exact transcript quote from [02:13]-[02:17] (transcript ground truth is $300, which covers the launch-price gold fact) and cover all 4 gold facts: flagship-class Snapdragon 800-series specs at roughly half the $600-700 rival price, the crazy-hard invite system creating scarcity plus million-unit buzz [02:49-02:58], and CyanogenMod's developer-friendly no-bloatware appeal [02:25-02:30]. A's citations all verified ([02:19] sandstone, [04:00-04:22] IP-rating/advertising tradeoffs, [08:48-09:04] reiteration, [13:31] 'ultimate pure enthusiast phone') and A adds a useful disambiguation that $330 and $250 belong to the OnePlus 2 and OnePlus X, not the One. B's one correctness slip: 'a third to half the flagship price' overstates the transcript, which supports only ~half ($300 vs $600-700); nothing in the video supports 'a third.' B partially offsets this with honest disclosure that no supplied frame shows the OnePlus One or its price, but the unsupported ratio is a strict-grading deduction A does not incur.

Both answers are accurate against the transcript with all spot-checked citations correct (A: [02:13]-[02:17] $300/Snapdragon, [10:51] 'Special. No More', [11:31] $900, [13:20]-[13:23] verdict; B: [02:17], [03:41]-[03:43], [11:56]-[11:58] Carl Pei, [13:24]-[13:27]). Both cover all 4 gold facts: 2014 $300 launch undercutting $600-700 flagships, invite system + enthusiast hype selling 1M+ units, price creep to $900 'like turning up the temperature on a stove' plus Nord/lineup sprawl eroding the identity, and the trajectory argument (flagship-killer origin to generic $900 carrier-store phone resembling Oppo, i.e., becoming what it disrupted). A wins narrowly on completeness: it includes the 'reads like ChatGPT' detail, the explicit two-peaks reconciliation, and ties the closing rumors back to the opening 'dismantled' headline, which B omits. Caveat on A: several highly specific visual claims (22M/3.3M view counts, 'Startup Phase 1' and '4 Realization' chapter cards, a Lifehacker headline at [00:34]-[00:45]) are unverifiable; none is contradicted by the transcript so no hard penalty, but they carry fabrication risk that B's more conservative, better-anchored visual claims do not.

Both correctly report the $300 launch price with the exact quote and citation [02:13]-[02:17] (the transcript says $300, not the annotators' $299/16GB, $349/64GB tiers, which the video never states, so $300 counts as covering that fact). Both cover all 4 gold facts: launch price; flagship-class specs (Snapdragon 800-series) at roughly half the $600-700 flagship price ('flagship killer', [02:32]-[02:39]); invite-only scarcity plus nuts pre-launch hype yet 1M+ sold ([02:09]-[03:02]); and CyanogenMod's developer-friendly, no-bloatware appeal ([02:23]-[02:30]). All spot-checked citations verify for both. Differentiators cancel out: A adds useful extras (the 'only enthusiasts would find it' nuance, the price-disambiguation vs the $330 OnePlus 2 and $250 OnePlus X) but leans on unverifiable frame claims (white back, Android 4.4.2 boot screen); B adds an honest disclosure that no supplied frame shows the OnePlus One or its price, but its 'a third to half the flagship price' framing slightly overstates the discount ($300 vs $600-700 is 43-50%, not a third).
